image_description:
### 1) Chart type
- **Scatter plot** (country-labeled points) with a **diagonal reference line** (y = x).

### 2) Layout techniques
- **Single panel** (no faceting/small multiples).
- No stacking; points are positioned in 2D space with **direct text labels**.
- Includes a **reference line** to compare whether values are above/below equality between the two axes.

### 3) Data variables

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| Country | Categorical (nominal) | Each point represents a country; **text label** next to point |
| Percentage of 40+ year-olds who say the world is getting better | Quantitative | **Position on x-axis** |
| Percentage of 15- to 24-year-olds who say the world is getting better | Quantitative | **Position on y-axis** |
| Equality reference (y = x) | Quantitative (derived/reference) | **Diagonal line** across plot indicating equal x and y values |

I wonder why everyone in Indonesia is so optimistic while the people in Mali are the most pessimistic. This relates to my community because we are the next generation and our optimism could be the thing keeping us from giving up on the Earth. If we think that this is going nowhere, we won't try to save our planet. I think this graph is trying to show us that the younger generations are more prepared to deal with the turmoil we are enduring currently because they have more optimism. I'd call this graph Optimism V. Age

raw_comment:
1) I notice that younger people in “first world countries” like US and France tend to have a more positive outlook on the world whereas older people in the same countries have a more negative perception. In “third world countries” like Zimbabwe and Ethiopia, both adults and young people alike have a positive outlook on the world.


2) It makes me wonder if privilege and quality of life affects your outlook on life and hopefulness for the future.


3) This relates to me and my community because as someone who grew up in America, I have a level of privilege that others unfortunately don’t. This affects my worldview and it’s important to take into account other peoples experiences and perspectives.


4) How People From Different Countries View Our Future

raw_comment:
I noticed that the younger age groups are typically the more optimistic ones when it comes to their views on how the state of the world is heading. This is particularly present in developed countries like the U.S and Japan. Indonesia, however, seems to be optimistic across all age groups, well ahead of the rest of the countries in the graph. 

I wonder what leads to the optimistic outlook when it comes to Indonesia. Why exactly do all age groups think the world is getting better with each generation? I also wonder why most people 40+ don't think this way. Is it some sort of bias because THEY aren't the new generation and people 15-24 are biased BECAUSE they are the new generation?

This relates to me and my community because as someone who is apart of this 'new generation', this is about my future and the state of the world as I grow up and live my life as an adult. World issues and mechanics like the economy, healthcare, and politics are going to be strongly influenced by this 'new generation' as I grow up.

A catchy headline would be "New Generations Breed New Hope"

One clarification I got from the reveal is that optimism in new generations is based on how people think childhood is changing and how newer generation grew up.

I notice the 15 to 24 year olds are more optimistic than wise and see that they think that each generation is getting better. I also notice older people in Brazil and Mali are saying that the new generation is not getting better and that things are worsening, and that Indonesia’s older group are saying that each generation is getting better and better.

I wonder why the older people have such low hopes for the newer generation and why they think it is getting worse. I wonder why a lot of the newer generation thinks they are going to be better and I wonder what would happen if the younger people and older people talked and see why the newer generation would do better and worse.

This relates to my community because it could mean many things like politics, economy and just health and life in general. Do the older people see something the newer generation does not and are we too optimistic? It is really hard to see both sides and there might be a lot of valid reasoning and meanings for each side.

raw_comment: I noticed a generational divide between youths and adults. When asked if the world is getting better with each generation, youth ages 15-24 seemed more optimistic than the 40+ especially within ‘first world' countries such as the US, UK, Japan and Germany. Only 3 of 20 countries have older generations who believe the world is improving which leads me to wonder if countries not mentioned believe it’s not improving at all. I wonder if people between ages 15-24 will have a different mindset as time goes on since the world keeps changing and what factors contribute to the populace’s perception on whether the world is becoming a better place. This relates to me and my community since I’m part of the youth and am surrounded by my age group at school but also have older cultured parents and family so I can see the different mindsets first hand between the youth and the older generation. I have noticed that older generations seem to make generalizations about the younger generations and that there is a common agreement among each side with many people between ages 15-24 finding themselves in line with each other about feeling the world being a better place as we progressively move forward with each generation. The Battle Between Generations: Where Is The World Headed?

I noticed that more people ages 15-24 said that the world is getting better with each generation whereas less people ages 40+ said that the world is getting better with each generation. Additionally, I noticed that there are many different countries with ranging economic, social and political statuses that participated in this survey.

I wonder how much the graph would change if every country in the world was included. Has the economic status affected these results? What was the reason for Indonesia and Mali having the highest/lowest results? What was the reason for young adults believing that the world is getting better with each generation?

This relates to me and my community because my age group is included in this graph. It’s interesting to see how optimistic the 15-24 age group is compared to the 40+ age group.

The graph depicts people ages 15-24 and 40+ and how they responded to the question that the world is becoming better/worse with each generation. A catchy headline I came up with was “How Optimistic is Your Generation?”

raw_comment: 1) As others have already pointed out, there seems to be a generational divide between youths and adults. In twelve of the twenty one surveyed countries, over 50% of youths (ages 15-24) demonstrated optimistic views. Adults, on the other hand, reported positive attitudes towards the future in only six countries. 2) The glaring question is why do older generations have a generally more negative mindset, while younger generations envision brighter futures? I wonder what factors contribute to the populace’s perception on wether the world is becoming a better place? Bie B. Sea from Taihu lake cited significant economic growth in Indonesia to account for its overall positivity. Nicky from Wuxi, China conjectured that societal pressures (for example the stresses of the education system) lead to negative outlooks. 3) I believe this information is very relevant to my community. In my speculation, growth mindsets are fundamental to progress. Positivity is an essential part of any healthy society, helping to motivate, inspire, and encourage its populace. I hope that my community and country will work towards improving the world for future generations, irrespective of their view of the future. 4) I would title this graph, “Age and Attitude: How Different Generations Perceive the Future”

raw_comment:
1. I notice that there is a linear pattern for this data, meaning that overall, the two age groups for each country share similar opinions for the future. For instance, in Indonesia, both age groups are the most optimistic of the others. On the other hand, Brazil and Mali are the two countries with the lowest percentages for both groups.
2. I wonder the factors that affected these countries’ views. How are the two age group so coordinated when it comes to their views on this topic? Also, I wonder how the younger generations (younger than 15) would respond to this study.
3. This reminds me of a lesson we had in Psychology, about the happiest countries in the world (I’m pretty sure Denmark came in first). I tend to find these psychology-related statistics interesting.
4. Looking on the Bright Side – Generational Optimism by Country

raw_comment:
1) As others have already mentioned, I too noticed that younger generations tend to be more optimistic concerning the future of their country, while adults generally held more negative views. 
In twelve of the twenty one countries surveyed, more than 50% of youths (ages 15-24) believed that the world was becoming a better place. The majority of adults (ages 40 and older) who were surveyed, demonstrated an overall positive mindset in only six of the twenty one countries. Obviously there is a disparity in world view between older and younger generations. 

2) The glaring question is, why do older generations have generally negative outlooks on life while younger generations perceive a brighter future? I wonder what factors contribute to the attitudes of a country’s populace? Bie B. Sea, from Taihu Lake, cited recent economic growth as a potential cause for Indonesia’s largely positive mindset. Others have pointed to societal pressures (for example stresses of the education system) as a reason for more negative mindsets.

raw_comment:
I notice that in general, more younger people ages 15-24 think that the world is getting better with each generation rather than older people ages 40+ do. I find this really interesting because often people think of younger generations as being especially pessimistic and negative, whereas this graph says otherwise.  The US is one of the farthest points away from the line, with a positive residual, meaning that more young people think the world is getting better, and less older people do. I wonder if what the world was like as the older people were growing up shaped how they feel about future generations - they might be negative because newer ideas and concepts that they are not used to are surfacing, and many people do not like change.
I wonder what other countries' data looks like - is there a specific reason that they chose to only survey/include these countries? If they included other countries, this graph might look completely different. I also wonder about the ages in between - how do 25-39 year olds feel about the world?
This relates to me and my community because I am in the younger generation, and I'm surprised at how positive so many young people are, because it often feels like the opposite.

raw_comment: The first thing I notice is that almost all countries surveyed that more younger people believe the world is getting better with each generation than older people. This could be due to the life-cycle effect that younger people have a more optimistic view of the world because to lack of experience. I also noticed that South American countries like Peru, Brazil, and Argentina are less optimistic overall than other countries. Meanwhile, many African countries like Zimbabwe, Ethiopia, Nigeria, and Kenya are more optimistic in general. One thing I wonder is why Indonesia is the strongest believer by in this graph by a large amount that the world is getter better with each generation. My title for this graph is "Percentage of believers that the world is improving generationally".

raw_comment: In this graph, I see that significantly more amount of young people, 15-24-year-olds, believe the world is getting better with each generation.  Two countries that I immediately notice have large differences between the opinions of the younger and older groups surveyed are the United States and Japan. For the United States, almost 65% of the younger generation believes the world is getting better with each generation while about 35% of the 40+ group believes this. For Japan, about 60% of the 15-24-year-old group believes the world is getting better with each generation while about 25% of the older group believes the prompt. Both countries have such vast changes with the younger groups' beliefs compared to the older. I wonder why these specific two countries have the largest differences. I also wonder why Indonesia's percentages are so high up for both groups, having over 75% for both. This relates to me and my community because my country, the U.S is part of this poll taken and my age is also considered in the graph, so it's interesting to see how my own generations view our progress of bettering the world. A title appropriate for this graph would be "Each Generations' Thoughts on the Bettering of the World."

raw_comment: In nearly all of the countries surveyed, more young people (aged 15-24) believed the world was getting better than middle aged to older people (ages 40 ). There were no significant outliers that had the opposite relationship. I also noticed that the differential between the two was greatest in countries that had relatively low population growth rates, like the United States and Japan. I learned in my human geography class that relationships like that can end out exacerbating issues for the older dependent population. I wonder if potentially that is one of the reasons why older people tend to have a more pessimistic viewpoint on where society is headed. I also wonder why some countries, like Indonesia, were so overwhelmingly positive in their views across the board, but some countries were overall very negative. I think it likely is influenced by the situation of internal affairs within that country. I’m not familiar with the political systems of each country here, but I think it’s also plausible for that to have a significant impact on people’s world views. This graph could be titled “Is the world improving with the new generation? They think so.”

raw_comment: I notice that in this graph, Indonesia has the highest percentage of older and younger people both agreeing that the world is getting better with each generation. raw_comment:
I’ve noticed on this graph that generally more people aged 15-24 believe the world is becoming better with each generation compared to people 40  years old believing the world is becoming better with new generations. This graph confuses me because it doesn’t really send a message or show a general trend for different country types, the only thing I really noticed was that there were more African and Asian countries that both had higher group percentages compared to European and South American countries. I’m curious as to why the author of this graph has chosen to only choose 21 countries and didn’t include a lot of age groups- because 15 year olds and 24 year olds are very very different people. It’s a very small data set too, with only 21,000 people taken in all of the countries. This graph relates to my community because there’s a stigma in my community and my country that more recent generations are “more lazy” than older generations, which might be the cause of the very low percentage of 40  year olds thinking the generations have been getting better. 

Does geography change the different generation’s views on each other?

This is relevant to me because I enjoy thinking about complex questions like these, and EVERYONE thinks about stuff like this once in a while. It is very important to our society to consider questions like these.

raw_comment: The graph showing plots of multiple countries and their older and younger population answering one question is the world getter better with each generation. I immediately noticed that the younger generations are a lot more optimistic than the older generation. The US for example has more than 50 percent of their 15-24 year olds believing that the world is getting better with each generation while less than 50 percent of the 40  year old believe the same thing. I am wondering based on this data could the political swing of one generation also be affecting their ideas about the future. In addition, could there be a discrepancy in what the older versus what younger populations believe is better for the world. This data is important and affects both my community and I due to the fact that the optimism in a community is really important when trying to advocate for anything. The large gap presented in the graph indicates that there are major differences between what the older and younger generations believe is better for the world. That is going to cause some tensions in a community such as mine if the discussion of differences is not addressed properly. A name I would give to this graph would be: Are the Kids Ruining Everything?

raw_comment:
I notice that the younger generation believes more that the world is getting better than their older counterparts. Countries like the United States and Japan have a large amount of disagreement across these generations. The location of each country does not seem to matter too much to the overall data, but there do seem to be clusters of European countries (Argentina, Spain, France, U.K.)  and African countries (Zimbabwe, Kenya, Ethiopia, Nigeria).

I wonder if the data would be different if the "older" generation (40+) was split into two or three sections.  What about people younger than 15 and people from 25 to 39 years old? Are we missing important information? I wonder how this data would contrast with a graph of how each country is doing economically. I wonder how it would contrast with a graph of which countries have different politics and ways of controlling society. How would it be different compared to news coverage and access to resources? This data is probably heavily influenced by the bias of each generation for themselves. I wonder if this data will increase or decrease in the future or if biases are the only factor.

This relates to me because I am in the younger generation and I hope that the world is getting better.

An article title could be: A Generational Divide: Are we really getting better?

raw_comment:
15- to 24-year-olds tend to say the world is getting better with each generation. I notice that there doesn't seem to be too much correlation between which countries are more positive or negative towards the future. It seems there are some countries from around the world, but this graph does not include many others like China or Korea, or Norway just to name a few.

I wonder if the older generation is generally less positive towards the future because they have lived longer and seen what the past was like and the present and future as less good. For example, a 50-year-old who has probably lived their childhood and adolescence without phones and computers and the internet might be more opposed to it than a 15-year-old who has probably lived most of their life with phones and the internet. I also wonder if older generations are generally more pessimistic or if they are just pessimistic towards the future of the world. In addition, I wonder what would this graph look like if it were taken 20 years ago, or 50, or 100? 

This relates to me and my community because I have noticed that my friends who fall in the 15- to 24-year-olds category do seem to be more open to change and more readily positive towards the future. However, adults around me are more neutral or slightly negative towards what the future holds. 

"The World is Getting Better, But for Who?"

image_description:
### 1) Chart type
- **Scatter plot** (country-labeled points) with a **diagonal reference line** (y = x).

### 2) Layout techniques
- **Single panel** (no faceting/small multiples).
- No stacking; points are positioned in 2D space with **direct text labels**.
- Includes a **reference line** to compare whether values are above/below equality between the two axes.

### 3) Data variables

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| Country | Categorical (nominal) | Each point represents a country; **text label** next to point |
| Percentage of 40+ year-olds who say the world is getting better | Quantitative | **Position on x-axis** |
| Percentage of 15- to 24-year-olds who say the world is getting better | Quantitative | **Position on y-axis** |
| Equality reference (y = x) | Quantitative (derived/reference) | **Diagonal line** across plot indicating equal x and y values |
raw_comment: I notice that many countries have a higher percentage of young people who are more optimistic about our world’s development, with elderly people showing less optimism. Especially in countries like the US, there is a huge difference, with about 70% of young people believing the world is getting better and only about 30% of the elderly believe it’s getting better. However there are some countries like Indonesia, India, and Nigeria who have relatively even numbers of people, young and old, who think that the world is getting better. I wonder why higher percentages of people in less developed countries say the world is getting better. Does it have anything to do with the fact that they see their own lack of development and then they see how the world around them has many improvements, and perceive improvements from around the world as much greater things than people from more developed countries might perceive them? This graph poses many questions about perspective, especially with the topic of improvement being very perspective based. This relates to my community because I am part of the younger generation, so it is very uplifting to see that large amounts of young people have very positive outlooks on the world. I would title this graph “Optimism: the new divide between generations”.

This graph is able to show the varying mindsets of relatively young people compared to relatively older people. It shows how younger people, on average, have a more positive mindset towards the way that the world has turned out.
The style of this graph is a scatter plot. The variables are the responses of the younger people and the responses of the older people. It shows a moderate correlation between young people and more positive responses towards the world's growing conditions. 
I feel that the audience intended for this graph are sociocultural and brain development professionals. From a psychological standpoint, this audience may be able to use this information to see the differences that different cultures' cognitive processes, regarding optimism, differ with age.
I think the purpose is to show the difference in cognition between different societies and ages. As younger people seem to have a more positive mindset, there may be a developmental stage during 15-24 years old that could impact your mindset. In addition, differences in culture can play a role in the differences in mental development.

raw_comment:
The first thing I noticed is how 'Young' people tend to lean towards the idea of "the world is getting better with each generation"  more than 'older' people. As in the graph, you can see that most of the 15 - 24-year-olds in the provided countries fall between the 50-75% range, with none under 25%. However, when looking at the Y-axis for people over the age of 40, most countries fall below 50%, and have a significant amount under 25%. 

There is not exactly a noticeable trend among the data, however you do notice that most of the '1st world' countries have a much higher 'optimistic' younger population than an older one. As you can see in the graph, the US, UK, Japan and Germany are all within the 50-75% range on the X-axis (younger people believing the world is getting better), meanwhile on the Y-axis (Older people believing that the world is getting better) all the previously mentioned countries fall below the 50% mark. 

There is an anomaly within this result, which would be Indonesia, as both the younger and older generations agree that the world is getting better as time goes on. This is likely due to the fact that Indonesia's economy has increased by over 600% since the year 2000, this would obviously see a great increase in the quality of life and general welfare of the populace. Under such conditions, it is quite understandable why Indonesia's population generally have such 'high hopes' for the future.

raw_comment: I've noticed that most countries that think the world is becoming a better place with each coming generation tend to be less wealthy than countries that think the world is becoming a worse place like Spain, France, and the United Kingdom. And I also noticed that Indonesia is a pretty big outlier, standing out from the rest by a decent amount. I wonder if that the reason many wealthier countries elder generation don't think that the world is becoming better with every generation is because of the wealth of that country or perhaps it is much deeper than that and is maybe due to how the older generation was raised and that they may fear change or think of change as a negative thing. In my community most teens including me think that yes the world is becoming a better place with every generation, but i couldn't really speak for the older generation.

raw_comment:
What do you notice

     In general, the young are more optimistic than the old, they think the world is getting better with each generation much more. There are very few countries listed that go below the dividing line (India, Nigeria, Morocco), and none that deviate far right and down from the dividing line. Indonesia has high percentages on both sides, while countries like Brazil and mail have low percentages.

What do you wonder

    I wonder what causes the younger generation to think more optimistically about the future. Why is Indonesia so high in both metrics but Brazil so low? Are countries that are currently in rapid development (like India) more likely to be higher on the scale?

How does this relate to you and your community 

    This relates to the United States because this chart shows younger people  are much more optimistic than older people. I’m not sure where this may come from, but it shows a generational divide about what “getting better” means to someone. 

 What’s going on in this graph? Create a catchy headline that captures the graph’s main idea

     A catchy headline would be: Your age and Your Optimism for the World

raw_comment: I can see from this chart that young people in most countries believe that the world is on a good path. But except for three countries, young people in other countries will be more optimistic about the development of the world than the elderly. But according to a psychological survey by UC Davis, 51 is the peak of optimism, but it doesn't seem to show this in this chart. This raises my question: is this data reliable enough? What is the specific age of the person who answered this question? But when we assume that the data of this questionnaire is reliable, a very interesting question arises. Can an optimistic attitude towards the development of the world really show that this person is an optimistic person? Because in my opinion, this is a very objective problem. I am in China because the epidemic protection is very strict. In China, entering shopping malls or some public areas requires a QR code to prove that they are healthy, but many middle-aged and elderly people are rejected because they don't have mobile phones or can't use them. This will make them feel abandoned, abandoned by the world. This is just a small example, but when we re-examine this chart, I believe everyone will have the answer.

raw_comment:
The first thing I notice is the clear lean in the graph that favors towards most countries having a higher percentage of 15-24-year-olds who are optimistic about the future of our world. I also notice that Morocco, Nigeria, and India are the only countries where this is not true, and the 40+-year-olds are most optimistic.

I wonder why this might be the case for these 3 countries. A generational change in world view? Is it politically, economically, or culturally based? The US and Ukraine have similar outlooks; however, they are very different counties. What might be common between these two countries that causes similar percentages?

This relates to my community and I through the idea that we are a data point on this graph. This graph can help us understand not only where we as a generation stand in terms out the future outlook, but where we as a country stand in respect to other countries. This may be able to shorten a generational gap, allowing us to put ourselves in our elders shoes and better understand their point of view.

This graph compares each countries percentage of 15-24 year olds who have an optimistic outlook on the future of our world, and the percentage of 40+ year olds who don't. A catchy title for this graph might be "The Generational Divide"

raw_comment:
What do you notice?
I notice the large division line separating the percentage of 15 to 24 year olds who say the world is getting better with each generation versus the percentage of 40+ year olds.  There is stark contrast between the generations portraying the difference in their optimism or hope. I also notice that they surveyed about 1,000 people from each of the countries listed.
What do you wonder?
I wonder how accurate this graph truly is. Considering that they are using 1,000 people to represent countries filled with millions upon millions of people, the margin of error has to be pretty large. I also wonder how much the generational effect holds true. This graph would go to prove how people that have seen more have less optimism, but I wonder why there are so many 15-24 year olds that are hopeless for the future generations. 
How does this relate to you and your community?
This relates to me and my community for I fit into the 15-24 year old category and am a member of Gen Z. If I were asked the same question, I would likely say that the world is not getting better with each generation because of certain things I have learned over the years. With that being said, I also do not think it has gotten worse. Almost the same, per say. 
What's going on in this graph?
"Gen Z: The Hopeful or Hopeless?"

A younger audience is denoted by someone who is from 15 to 24 years old while the older audience is denoted by someone who is older than 40 years of age. The positive line of 1 shows equal thinking of both age groups. As a country gets further and further from the line, there is a larger disparity between the thinking of younger generations vs older ones. A catchy title would be “The surprising reality of optimism around the world.”

raw_comment: I noticed that there are way more countries with a higher percentage of younger people that are saying that the world is getting better each generation. I also noticed that Indonesia has such a high percentage of people that think in both directions. It's important to know that younger people are more optimistic about future generations rather than the old people. I think that old people just think younger generations are annoying and unpatriotic, while younger people read history books and see how there were wars and bad things going on previously to their generation. I wonder why some countries are not very optimistic and some are. Is it the number of old people in a country, the economic stability, or is it something else? This relates to me and my community because being optimistic about our future generations is very important, and in the U.S. we are pretty optimistic mainly in the younger ages. A catchy title could be "Excited for the future, maybe not."

Title: Losing Optimism As You Get Older (Charlie, Linnea, Amy)

We notice that 15-24 year olds are more optimistic that the world is getting better and the 40 year olds are seeing the opposite. Indonesia has the highest population out of all the countries. Also, we wonder what the older people see as the negatives about the world and what the youngers see so postive. The impact we think affects us is because we, ourselves, are apart of the 15-24 year olds.

raw_comment: The first thing I notice is that the 15-24 year-olds in the majority of the countries surveyed responded in greater numbers that the world is getting better with every generation in comparison to people who are 40+ in their country. I also noticed that Indonesia overall has a much higher rating of people saying the world gets better with every generation and this was across the board for ages surveyed. I also observed that the closer each country's dot was to the black line, the more the two age groups agreed with the other. For example, Morocco, Nigeria, and India all show that these two groups have the relative same consensus. I saw that about 21,000 and up people have been interviewed in each country by phone. I wonder if certain areas of these countries have different overall opinions and if they contrast from those in different areas. Also, if the survey was conducted only by telephone it could impact the results of the people being surveyed if many people in a country do not have access to a phone. I think a good name for the graph would be: "The Older and Younger Generations Hope for the World"

raw_comment:
1.	I notice that, the younger generation in this data, aged 15-24, is more optimistic about the future because they believe that life is improving with each generation. To analyze this graph, the X-axis is represented for more than 40 years old, and the Y-axis is represented for the age of 15 to 24. This graph is including 21 different countries. According to the data, Indonesia has the highest score, and Mali has the lowest score. Indonesia is the only country in which more than 75% aged 15 to 24, population believes the world is improving.The countries which optimistic in both age groups are usually LICs such as Nigeria and Ethiopia. The countries with higher HDI have a higher percentage of younger generations which the world is getting better than more than 40+ generations. Besides, people aged 25 to 39 were not involved in this survey.

2.	I wonder why they excluded the age group of 25-39 in this survey and why did Mali get the lowest point? Why older people are less optimistic than younger people? 

3.	I believe this graph is relevant because since my nation is not included in this survey and I haven’t thought about whether the world is becoming a better or worse place compared with generation. However, in my opinion among each generation, we could make a better future. This graph pleases my interest in learning more about our thought processes and determining whether further developments are achievable.

4. "Is our future getting better or worse?"

raw_comment: The graph compares the younger vs older generations faith in future generations to better the world.  I notice the younger group of people have a more positive outlook on the future of the world and the impact new generations have than the older generations. Infact, with the exception of India, Nigeria, and Moroco every group of younger people have more faith than their older counterparts. I wonder if this disparity in faith is simply old people being pessimistic, or if it is a result of an overconfident younger generation that lack the wisdom to realistically look into the future. This doesn’t really relate to my community, regardless of our faith in future generations both current generations young and old will strive to better the world, thus this comparison is no more than an insightful insight into our faith in the future.

raw_comment:
Graph Name: "The generational views on the future of our planet"

We notice how the 15 to 24-year-old age range upholds more optimism in society and views the progress in the world in a more positive way. The slim percentage of people 40+ who view the world in an optimistic way were most commonly from African countries with a slim percentage coming from India. We wonder why the younger age group is more optimistic than the older age group and how the development of each country voting may/may not play an impact on how they view the world as well. As students who all fall into the 15-24 age range, it’s interesting to see how people like us view the world. It’s impactful to see that there is hope and optimism in our generation and in the future generations to come, even through all of the hardships the world is currently facing.

- **Scatter plot** (country-labeled points) with a **diagonal reference line** (y = x).

### 2) Layout techniques
- **Single panel** (no faceting/small multiples).
- No stacking; points are positioned in 2D space with **direct text labels**.
- Includes a **reference line** to compare whether values are above/below equality between the two axes.

### 3) Data variables

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| Country | Categorical (nominal) | Each point represents a country; **text label** next to point |
| Percentage of 40+ year-olds who say the world is getting better | Quantitative | **Position on x-axis** |
| Percentage of 15- to 24-year-olds who say the world is getting better | Quantitative | **Position on y-axis** |
| Equality reference (y = x) | Quantitative (derived/reference) | **Diagonal line** across plot indicating equal x and y values |
raw_comment:
Thanks to all the students who responded. Here are some of the catchy headlines that we received: "The Opposing Futures in the Eyes of Different Generations" by Helena of Pewaukee High School and “The Ages of Optimism” by Zoe, both from Wisconsin, "Generation Battle: Is the World Getting Better or Worse?" by Taim of Gladeville Middle School in Mt. Juliet, Tennessee. 

You can continue to post responses.  Please check back by Friday, Feb. 11 to see the Reveal with a link to the original New York Times article, some questions about the graph, and a few Stat Nuggets.

Please visit our archive to see all graphs in this series (tinyurl.com/NYTGraphs).

View our archives that link to all past releases, organized by topic (tinyurl.com/NYTGraphsTopicIndex), graph type (tinyurl.com/NYTGraphsTypeIndex) and Stat Nugget (tinyurl.com/NYTGraphsStatNuggetsIndex). 

Signing off – Sharon Hessney of Boston, who sees hope in the optimism of our students, and Dashiell Young-Saver of San Antonio, who's become more optimistic about the world after seeing young people's optimism about the world.

raw_comment:
This graph shows how different generations and age groups view the future of world. The younger generation (ages 15-24) are more optimistic about the future of world and believe that the world is getting better with each generation. I noticed that in Indonesia both young adults and older adults are optimistic for the future of the world. I wonder what caused the older generations to lose their faith for the future in the world. I also wonder if the younger adults are biased since they are considered to be the future of the world. This affects my community because I am in the younger generation. I think the future of the world will be bright.
Name for Graph: "Age VS Optimism"

raw_comment: I noted that the percentage of younger generations is more significant in practically every country when compared to over 40-year-olds. This is intriguing because it demonstrates how the younger generation is more optimistic about their future than previous generations. I wonder if this might be due to both groups' prejudice towards themselves. Within their age, younger individuals are more confident. On the other hand, older adults feel they are better at controlling the world. This relates to my community because we are around these regions. A title could be Optimistic generations of young adults.

raw_comment:
Young adults have optimism for the future.
We noticed that 15-24-year-olds have more hope for the future. The countries that the 40+ age group has the most optimism is India, Indonesia, and Nigeria. It is obvious that the younger generations have more optimism about the future of our society. 
Something that we were wondering about is why do these young adults think the world is getting better. We wonder why countries like Brazil and Mali have little to no optimism about the future. We think it might be because they are living a poor life right now so why would they have hope for the future. We also wonder about what people are thinking when they answer this question. For example, do they think that the world will become more peaceful or will their singular life get better?
This topic impacts us because we are a part of the 15-24-year-old group and it is interesting to see that a lot of people our age have hope for the future. It is also fascinating to see what the 40+ group thinks of the future.

raw_comment: The top left of the graph is labeled on if 15-24-year-olds think the world is getting better with each generation and it states 40 year olds’ opinions on just if the world is getting better. We also noticed that country’s socioeconomic condition didn’t determine its belief on if the world is getting better. There was great variation across all countries with different socioeconomic conditions. Why were these countries chosen when some of them are really small and some of them are very big? Why did they choose certain age groups from certain countries but not evaluate both age groups in the countries? The graph displays that about 65% of the US views each generation is improving. About 35% wouldn’t believe that though in the U.S.. Our societal issues drive the beliefs whether or not the generations are getting better. Additionally, the US is a first world country which can allow for a more positive outlook. Our catchy headline for this statistics set would be: World for Better or Worse?

raw_comment:
We noticed that younger groups are more optimistic and the older group is less optimistic. We noticed that there is a diagonal line separating adolescents and adults. We know this because there are more dots on the younger side. We wonder why the country selection is seemingly random. Does the country's wealth affect optimism? How accurate is the data perceived in the graph? If the data is accurate that means it's good that the younger people are more optimistic because they are the leading generation. What determines how far a dot is on the x-axis? We wonder why there is such a difference and what the leading opinions for the 15-24 year-olds are vs the 40+ year-olds. What makes other countries less optimistic than others? How does the younger generation's impact and involvement in society affect how they and others view the world? If we were to rename this graph we would name it "The Optimism of the Younger Generation." 

 Max, Grace, Mac, and Cierra - "Wordle Gods"

image_description:
### 1) Chart type
- **Scatter plot** (country-labeled points) with a **diagonal reference line** (y = x).

### 2) Layout techniques
- **Single panel** (no faceting/small multiples).
- No stacking; points are positioned in 2D space with **direct text labels**.
- Includes a **reference line** to compare whether values are above/below equality between the two axes.

### 3) Data variables

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| Country | Categorical (nominal) | Each point represents a country; **text label** next to point |
| Percentage of 40+ year-olds who say the world is getting better | Quantitative | **Position on x-axis** |
| Percentage of 15- to 24-year-olds who say the world is getting better | Quantitative | **Position on y-axis** |
| Equality reference (y = x) | Quantitative (derived/reference) | **Diagonal line** across plot indicating equal x and y values |
raw_comment:
As a team, we noticed that there is an upward trend in the graph. It seems that 15-24 year olds tend to be more optimistic than 40+ year olds. Additionally, there is not a strong correlation between how developed a country is and their positivity; some developed countries are quite pessimistic, and some developing countries are very optimistic. 
We wondered why people in the 15-24 year old range were so much more optimistic than 40+ year olds on average? We also wondered what would make people in some of the developing countries so much more optimistic than the people in some of the developed countries, and if it might be because the people in developing countries were seeing positive change and an improvement in the quality of life, while the people in developing countries are not seeing as drastic and noticeable positive changes. 
We thought that a catchy title could be “Is The World Improving? 15-24 Year Olds Think So.”

raw_comment:
After examining the graph, I noticed that the percentage of younger generations is higher in almost every country compared to 40+ year olds when asked if the world is getting better with each generation. This is interesting because it shows how the younger generation has more optimism for themselves compared to older generations. This could be because of a bias towards themselves for both groups. The younger people have more confidence within their own generation. Whereas the older people believe that they are better at managing the world.

I wonder what specifically gave the people of Indonesia the confidence in their younger generation compared to the rest of the world. With the percentage overall being much higher compared to all other countries, I wonder if there is a specific cause to this statistic.

This relates to my community because I have noticed that much of the older generation in my community is much more hesitant towards being confident in the younger generation for the future compared to the young people themselves. Whether this be due to the hope of still being young, or another cause. It is something very prevalent in my community.

This graph shows the comparison of percentage of young adults vs 40+ year olds who believe that the world is becoming better with each generation. A catchy headline could be, Do older generations have hope in new generations?

raw_comment: This graph serves to compare adolescents (15-24) and adults (40+) and their level of optimism for the future of their country. I noticed in the graph that typically the younger generations had more optimism. This is because the majority of the 15-24 year old responses in the graph were above the line that was used to show how similar the younger and older adults' answers were for each country. For example, India's point is very close to the line, which therefore shows that India's adolescents and older adults have a very similar response. Along with this information, I also noticed that the countries with the highest optimism were countries that are still developing and are continuously growing and improving. I wonder why Brazil and Mali are so low on the graph. I also wonder what determines levels of optimism. I want to know why adults are generally less optimistic. Could this be because they have more life experience and have more realistic expectations? This relates to me and my community because I am in high school and I  live in the United States. I would say that the information in the graph accurately reflects my country. A good title for this graph could be: "Country Optimism Through the Ages"

raw_comment:
This graph offers incite about how different generations view the future of the world. It asks 15-24 and 40+ year olds is the world is getting better with each generations. This graph depicts many more countries such as Brazil, Japan, the US and Zimbabwe showing 15-24 year olds saying the world gets better with each generation. On the opposing side, few countries such as Nigeria, India and Morocco have adults over the age of 40 agreeing the world gets better with each generation. Within theses graphs you can also see the percentage of people that are in agreement. Mali and Brazil, for example, both had a little more than 25% agree the world is getting better. I wonder why these countries are so low and why countries like Ethiopia and Zimbabwe are much more in favor of this question. Being in the 15-24 year old generation I think me and my friends have more faith in the younger generations than my parents generation, who still have more than my grandparents generation. It causes me wonder about if your lack of faith in the next generation lowers over time. 

Catchy Headline: "Generational Effect"

raw_comment:
1. I noticed that out of all of the countries, there were only 3 countries whose adults over 40 years old believe that the world is getting better. But even then, only 45-60% of adults in these countries actually believe this. 

Indonesia is the only country where over 75% of its population of 15-24 year olds things the world is getting better. The rest of the countries' percentage of believers range from anywhere between 25%-75%. 

2. I wonder how human development influenced these results. Around 15-24 years of age, you slowly but surely start to lose your innocence as more terrible aspects of the world become clear. That may be why so few people in that age range actually believe that the world can experience a positive change. The older you get, however, you will witness so many changes since the years you were young, especially technological and medical advancements making life more convenient for others. 

The graph also shows that this works the other way. The greater abundance of countries whose 15yr-24yr population have high faith in the future may have to do with youthful thinking and hope before it gets shattered with more experience (most likely corresponding with more years of living). 

3. The age range of people in my town is a bit over 40 years, and this makes me intrigued to learn more about our ways of thinking and view on if future improvements are feasible. 

4. "Has Hope For Our Future Decreased? Here's 40 Countries' Input for Why and Why Not"

raw_comment:
I wonder... Since the survey gathered their results by telephone, are the results skewed? The type of survey forces a voluntarily response, as people will only share their thoughts about whether or not they believe the world is becoming a better place, if they are up to answering the phone. So do people willing to answer the phone have a different and more collective opinion as opposed to people who aren't willing to respond? 

I noticed... A majority of the responses of the younger generation fall between the 25% and 75% range. Though it may indicate that people are collectively thinking the world is getting better, it also shows a range of responses. This raises questions as to what factors makes the certain countries opinion at a specific point. 

Community... The U.S is higher on the graph than I would expect, as I always hear people my age saying "I wish we lived in our parents or grandparents era." 

Title: The Ages of Optimism

Based on the graph, 15-24-year-olds are more optimistic than people who are 40 and older.
I wonder if there is some inaccuracy.
I do not think that people can really concept how the world was with the older generation.
The world is changing so much so fast.
It would be hard to determine if the world is becoming better.
You know major events that happen during history during that time.
If you did not live through that point in time then you cannot really know.
I wonder if younger people are more optimistic because they have been raised with the internet.
The internet gives younger people more hope and belief that the world will become better.
I ask if more younger people think the world is becoming better based the economic situation in their country.
I wonder how the graph would look different if you surveyed the 40 and older generation when they were younger.
I want to see if their answers would differ.
A catchy catch phrase is "Countries Optimistic Generation".

image_description:
### 1) Chart type
- **Scatter plot** (country-labeled points) with a **diagonal reference line** (y = x).

### 2) Layout techniques
- **Single panel** (no faceting/small multiples).
- No stacking; points are positioned in 2D space with **direct text labels**.
- Includes a **reference line** to compare whether values are above/below equality between the two axes.

### 3) Data variables

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| Country | Categorical (nominal) | Each point represents a country; **text label** next to point |
| Percentage of 40+ year-olds who say the world is getting better | Quantitative | **Position on x-axis** |
| Percentage of 15- to 24-year-olds who say the world is getting better | Quantitative | **Position on y-axis** |
| Equality reference (y = x) | Quantitative (derived/reference) | **Diagonal line** across plot indicating equal x and y values |

raw_comment: This graph compares the percentages of 15-24-year-old and 40+-year-olds on their view of whether the world is getting better with each generation. The younger generation is far more optimistic and most believe that the world is getting better with each generation. However, the older generation is not as confident in whether or not the world is getting better with each generation. The world is so crazy right now, and so people would hope that everyone is at least wishing that the world is getting better with each generation. I wonder what the younger generations have to do to help persuade the older generation into thinking that they can make the world better. I also wonder how these percentages are going to change as the years go on. This relates to my community because I am a part of the younger generation and many teenagers believe that the world is getting better with each generation. A catchy headline is "Is Each Generation Making The World Better?"

raw_comment: What I find both interesting and predictable is that in most countries, there are more 15-24-year-olds than 40+-year-olds who think that the world is getting better with each generation. It took me a second to recognize, but the countries on the top left side of the line (positive residual) have higher percentages of younger adults than older adults who believe that the world is getting better, while on the bottom right (negative residual) show that the older adults think that the world is getting better more than, the younger adults. It makes sense that older people wouldn't believe that the world is improving since there are more progressive young adults and conservative older adults. This leads me to wonder, how would these statistics change as time continues to progress and younger adults become older adults? Since new concepts are built each generation, it makes sense that those older than the latest generation would have a hard time understanding how the world is changing and thus not want it to change. This is important to remember to ensure that the world truly continues to get better and that all generations can be excited and confident about those changes.

raw_comment:
The most evident feature of this graph is that the amount of people aged 15-24 believe that the world gets better with each generation is very large, compared to the other portion of the graph, describing 40+ year olds. The second thing that I noticed was that the countries inside of the 40+ boundary are not in Europe or the Americas, but in Africa and Asia. Does that show that middle aged adults are more confident in their successors compared to the west? Another thing that I noticed is that the younger generations feel much better about themselves versus the older generations. The disconnect between young people and older people about the future is more alarming than anything else.

The biggest thing that I wonder is how the younger generation is so optimistic, given that many of their key development years have been distorted due to the pandemic. Adults have had the privilege to go through decades of their lives without worrying the virus, so naturally shouldn't they be more hopeful that things would go back to normal?

This affects me and my community because my life has been effected because of growing up in the pandemic. As a member of the 15-24 year old group, I can say that we've been able to find hope and happiness despite all of the negative things that have been happening in the world. I think it's important for the older generations to find some too.

raw_comment: This scatter plot, representing the data between two discrete variables with moderate positive correlation, shows how young adults (ages 15-24 years old) are more optimistic than 40+ years old adults. The audience can be psychologists, therapists, counselors, or anyone concerned about mental health, directing them towards pessimistic adults and offering help. The meaning of this plot can mean the world is becoming a better place because present young adults didn’t have to experience as much pain or suffering the 40+ years old adults did when they were younger. Another takeaway could be that older people have learned the negativity in our world through life experiences; hence, they are less optimistic. For instance, young adults could have big or unrealistic dreams and hopes for their future career, lifestyle, income, etc. But as they age, realizing the realities of how the real world works, it's easy to see why the older adults are less optimistic. The purpose of this plot is to compare and contrast how two different groups of people view our world, providing data for anyone interested.

raw_comment:
Welcome to Wednesday's live moderation of “What’s Going On in This Graph?”  

Today’s graph relates the optimism between generations about the future for people from 21 countries.

From 9 am – 2 pm E.T. today, share with us your responses online to these three questions -- "What do you notice?",  "What do you wonder?"  and “How will this affect you?”  Plus, please share with us a catchy headline that captures the ideas in this graph. 

By Friday morning, we’ll reveal the article that included this graph and much more.  We look forward to reading your discoveries.

Note that you can get a link to your response to share with your teacher and classmates by clicking the response box below your response “Email me when my comment is published.”  

Please visit our archive to see all graphs in this series (tinyurl.com/NYTGraphs).
View our archives that link to all past releases, organized by topic (tinyurl.com/NYTGraphsTopicIndex), 
graph type (tinyurl.com/NYTGraphsTypeIndex) and 
Stat Nugget (tinyurl.com/NYTGraphsStatNuggetsIndex).  

The next “What’s Going On In This Graph?” will be released by Friday, Feb. 11 with moderation on Wednesday, Feb. 16.  The topic – weddings!  Keep noticing and wondering.

raw_comment: The scatter graph shows the comparison of generation z(15-24) and generation x(40+), clearly with the younger generation of the sample being more optimistic towards improvement in the world. This graph would help us and geographers understand some people's views, which could lead to further research into why the younger generations have a positive prospect on their generation. It's indisputable that for even the most pessimistic country, Mali, the younger generation of the sample agrees that the world is improving. Why would the younger generations be so optimistic, why would the older generation be so pessimistic? There are many plausible reasons for this, firstly, the younger generation is born into a relatively more privileged and peaceful era, the worst a gen z could go through is the SARS or COVID-19 epidemics. I would say that most gen z's aren't hit by reality yet, the capacity for a gen z to think broad and critically is inferior to a gen x, the youth has baseless ambitions for future success, or rather, hasn't been hit by reality yet. Gen x is more pessimistic as they have most likely been hit with life and given up on some, they do not have the important resource - youth, health - that gen z has. This graph does show good news, improvement of any scale requires people with ambitions, and this graph ensures us that gen z's have the confidence of creating a better world.

raw_comment:
1. I notice that the X axis is for the responses of 40+ year olds, and the Y axis is for the responses of people age 15-24. The data is from a survey in 2021 that included 21 countries. Indonesia has the highest score on this graph, and Mali has the lowest. In the US a larger percentage of people age 15-24 think the world is getting better than people age 40+.

2. I wonder why people age 25-39 were not included on the graph. I wonder why this graph chose to show the difference in opinion between different age groups instead of showing the overall percent by country.

3. This relates to me and my community because the US was included in this survey. I am not surprised that a larger percent of young people have a more positive outlook than people age 40 or older.

4. “Are young people more optimistic about the world?”

image_description:
### 1) Chart type
- **Scatter plot** (country-labeled points) with a **diagonal reference line** (y = x).

### 2) Layout techniques
- **Single panel** (no faceting/small multiples).
- No stacking; points are positioned in 2D space with **direct text labels**.
- Includes a **reference line** to compare whether values are above/below equality between the two axes.

### 3) Data variables

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| Country | Categorical (nominal) | Each point represents a country; **text label** next to point |
| Percentage of 40+ year-olds who say the world is getting better | Quantitative | **Position on x-axis** |
| Percentage of 15- to 24-year-olds who say the world is getting better | Quantitative | **Position on y-axis** |
| Equality reference (y = x) | Quantitative (derived/reference) | **Diagonal line** across plot indicating equal x and y values |
raw_comment:
1) This graph is based on people's opinions hence, some inferences can be made on the trend. One thing that I notice is that countries near to the top right corner of the graph (where both more adults and young adults agree that the world is getting better with each generation) are mostly third world and developing countries. I inferred that people are likely more satisfied with the newer generations in devloping and third world countries as these types of countries grow faster compared to devloped countries because they are starting at a lower point. The faster the devlopment, the more satisfied the people are with the newer generations. 
2) I wonder what other factors about the count ries are majorly impacting the data distribution 
3) This relates to me as I am a part of the 15-24 year old range. I personally believe that the world is getting better with each generation. The data also supports my claim as most of the data point are above the solid black line. This indicates that more people in my age range agree with me than people in the 40+ age range. This data also relates to my community as a lot of people my age which I have asked also agree with me.
4) The graph is basically comparing adults and young adult's opinions on the new generation compared to the old generations. The heading which I would create for this graph would be“What does the present think about the future?”

### 2) Layout techniques

### 3) Data variables

raw_comment:
I have noticed that this graph is a stacked scatter plot, which consists of participants from 7 African countries, 6 Asian countries, 4 American countries, and 4 European countries. The survey was fairly distributed (based on diversity) in these aspirations of generations bettering the world as time progresses. I have also noticed the lack of 40+ year-old participants, which I perceive as a sign that their opinion is rather less significant in this topic, because their generation has fundamentally passed. I believe that 15–24-year old’s are more reliable in this survey, because they are the current generation and could be considered as people who still have time to take new steps to better the world.  I am genuinely fascinated, and wonder how people respond to this question. What is the criteria of ‘better’? Doesn’t their environment affect the way they perceive the world? Due to this, this graph in my perspective is unreliable, these countries are in completely different circumstances (whether it’s in terms of GDP, environment, health, safety etc.). Therefore, ‘better’ is complete bias. Different standards are incompatible. Coming from myself, a citizen of the poorest country in the world, Burundi, my standards of ‘better’, definitely can’t be similar to the standards of someone from one of the richest countries in the world (USA).  One would benefit from this ‘bettering’ and another may find it as a disadvantage. 

My Headline: IS THE FUTURE ‘BETTER’ FOR EVERYONE?

raw_comment: This graph is to show the comparison of young adults (15 to 24-year-olds) and middle-aged adults (40-year-old) and their amount of optimism for the future. The results highlight that the younger generations are much more optimistic about themselves and their future world, whilst the older generation believes that there is little hope for the future. This is concerning as it is worrying that the adults are so negative about our future, possibly meaning that they see a future that younger generations cannot see. I also observed that countries that are lower in socio-economical status and rely more on religion, such as Ethiopia, India and Indonesia, have a significantly higher percentage of young and old generations of adults with beliefs of improvement for our future generations.

- **Scatter plot** (country-labeled points) with a **diagonal reference line** (y = x).

### 2) Layout techniques
- **Single panel** (no faceting/small multiples).
- No stacking; points are positioned in 2D space with **direct text labels**.
- Includes a **reference line** to compare whether values are above/below equality between the two axes.

### 3) Data variables

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| Country | Categorical (nominal) | Each point represents a country; **text label** next to point |
| Percentage of 40+ year-olds who say the world is getting better | Quantitative | **Position on x-axis** |
| Percentage of 15- to 24-year-olds who say the world is getting better | Quantitative | **Position on y-axis** |
| Equality reference (y = x) | Quantitative (derived/reference) | **Diagonal line** across plot indicating equal x and y values |
raw_comment:
The graph clearly shows a larger percentage of people aged 15-24 think the world is getting better. Third world countries have greater optimism with the world getting better hopefully meaning that these countries are receiving more support or are developing at a faster rate. The older you get the less optimistic you get about the world which shows that experiences have a lot to say about the way that you view the earth. The more experiences you have, the more negative your thoughts are which can show that the world isn't actually getting better and you could conclude that people aged 15-24 have less leverage due to their lack of experience and existence on the face of the earth. Younger generations have not seen the world change as much as older people which means that people aged 40+ are able to make a clearer judgement when it comes to the world getting better. However, there is no single generation that conclusively thinks the world is getting better which clearly shows that personal experiences (that do not relate to the world as a whole) have a massive effect on your judgement of the question. 

I wonder whether we can ever say that the world is getting better. Will there ever be a time when that statement is true?

Growing up as Gen Z, I have seen the personalities of other peers and I can say that Gen Z is a forward-thinking generation that is inclusive of all people. This can help the world be better to a certain extent.

raw_comment:
What do you notice?
Like many others I noticed that young people seem a lot more hopeful in terms of the future however more specifically people in what I imagine to be "developing countries" everyone is a lot more hopeful than the general first world countries where adults don't seem as hopeful. 

What do you wonder?
As a child we have grown up in the age of technology meaning we are surrounded by the media. In my experience this means being surrounded with statements about the world dying due to climate change or a political debate or the earth splitting into millions of pieces. However I have never felt threatened by this so I wonder why young adults are not loosing hope about our future. How are we not more influenced by the media into losing hope for the future?

How does this relate to you and your community?
Although Australia isn't on the graph I still find myself relating to the data. I think some older people in the community are loosing hope and going further perhaps"giving up". They are not going of be around for all of our future therefore it is not as relevant.

raw_comment:
What do you notice?
From looking at the graph, you can see that often young adults are more hopeful about the future in comparison to people aged over 40. I also see that it is not extremely dependent on the geographical location of the country as the dots are all mixed and range a lot. 

What do you wonder?
I am curious about what these people thought about as they answered these questions. I wonder what they thought defined "the world getting better". I wonder if they thought about their own individual lives or more deeply about the world as a whole. 

How does this relate to you and your community?
From this graph, we are able to learn about individuals across the world as well as their views about how the world is changing. This can make it clearer as to how young adults and older adults are viewing the world, and even suggest why the world is changing in such ways. I can learn about my community and how it may want to change the world or keep it the same. This graph can suggest how my community, and many others, want to change the world or suggest how young adults may make choices that are varying to older adults. 

What’s going on in this graph? Create a catchy headline that captures the graph’s main idea.
Youth waiting to change the world

raw_comment: Interestingly, I notice that this graph gives insight into social values and experiences from people all around the world. It shows us that the older demographic of people see more "ugly" in the world and believe that this will not change as time goes on. The correlation between developed countries and their percentage of 40+-year-olds is also interesting as it gives an indication that conflict experienced in developing countries may give a reason for those in developing countries to think that the world will not get better.

raw_comment:
1.	Overall, the young generation aged 15 to 24 years old in this data are more optimistic into the future as they believe the world is becoming a better place with each generations. In contrast, the people who have taken the survey aged 40+ have a higher percentage in believing the world is turning worse with each generations. 
The countries who are both optimistic in both age group are usually the developing countries, which are advancing their society in a rapid speed, as shown in the graph, Nigeria, Kenya, Ethiopia and Simbabwe. While countries who use to have a glorious history of successful in the globe, yet no longer holds their position do not believe the country is becoming better with each generation. For example, Napoleonic period for France and the Golden Age for the UK. However, there are 2 outliers on the bottom right of the chart, which is Brazil and Mali, where both countries never had a period of leading the world and the status quo does not allow the citizen to see a bright future.
2.	What are the marking guidelines for people in judging if a world is becoming better or worse?
3.	This data does not includes my country, which does not relate to me directly.
4.	"Is our world getting better?"

raw_comment:
1. What do you notice?
I noticed that this graph breaks down peoples ages and surveys whether or not they think that generations to come will make the world a better place or not. I also noticed that people aged 25-39 were not surveyed. 
2. What do you wonder?
I wonder why Indonesia’s results are so high and why Brazil and Mali's results are so low. I wonder if their location impacted their results. I also wonder if these results will change in the future or how these results would have compared to the past. 
3. What impact does this have on you and your community?
This impacts me because I am in the 15-24 year old category and I think that the world is getting better with each generation. Also, I think that my community is very optimistic so they will do everything in  their power to make changes so the world can be a better place. 
4. What's going on in this graph? Write a catchy headline that captures the graph's main idea.
In this graph, we see a graph that displays how different aged people think whether or not the world is getting better with each generation. Headline: “Will the world get better with time? Or will the younger generations be our downfall?

I wonder how these results would have compared to the past.
This impacts me.
I am in the 15-24 year old category.

I think that my community is very optimistic.

In this graph, we see a graph.

The headline is:

Or will the younger generations be our downfall?

image_description:
### 1) Chart type
- **Scatter plot** (country-labeled points) with a **diagonal reference line** (y = x).

### 2) Layout techniques
- **Single panel** (no faceting/small multiples).
- No stacking; points are positioned in 2D space with **direct text labels**.
- Includes a **reference line** to compare whether values are above/below equality between the two axes.

### 3) Data variables

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| Country | Categorical (nominal) | Each point represents a country; **text label** next to point |
| Percentage of 40+ year-olds who say the world is getting better | Quantitative | **Position on x-axis** |
| Percentage of 15- to 24-year-olds who say the world is getting better | Quantitative | **Position on y-axis** |
| Equality reference (y = x) | Quantitative (derived/reference) | **Diagonal line** across plot indicating equal x and y values |
raw_comment:
Ignorance is Bliss?

 **What do you notice?**

I notice that in general, young people, aged 15-24 are more optimistic about the future than older people aged over 40. In developed countries of greater wealth, the divide between the youth and over 40s is more significant compared to the difference in less wealthy countries. Surprisingly, the quality of life in the countries doesn’t seem to have a clear relationship with the optimism of its people, with several countries that have a large percentage of its people in poverty or in conflict ranking higher than that of the USA, Germany, Japan and France. 

- **What do you wonder?**

I wonder why Indonesian youth adults are so positive about the future in comparison to other countries and whether this has to do with their quality of life, culture and government system. I also wonder what causes people, in general, to become less hopeful for the future when they are older, and whether the hopes of young people are misplaced and a result of naive idealism and the belief that they have the power to change the future for the better. Is it because older people have less faith in the younger generations that they believe that things will get worse?

raw_comment: This scatter plot with a minimal pearsons correlation between the two variables (15-to-24-year-olds and adults) was made to show the contrasting attitudes whereby younger people are more optimistic than adults. Its audience could cover a wide variety of people. Because the causation for such a phenomenon could be analyzed through multiple lenses or theories. The meaning of this graph encompasses a much more sinister message for younger audiences under the age of 24. As it seems with the graph, the older you get, the more you understand how ugly the world is, and therefore why they think the world is not getting better. A relevant example is as housing prices soar, median salaries stagnate, it is easy to see why the common person can be optimistic when they are young, hoping to be millionaires, but once realizing the realities of our current economy, it can be depressing. The purpose of this graph is to explain the contrasting psychology in younger and older people and how they view the world. It could be an exploration into how we change our perceptions over time.

In addition, the graph shows that younger people are more optimistic about the world than older people. There are some points that show that old generation is positive, however, when looking at the average figure, this is usually the case for the younger generation. 

I wondered what factors made the distribution different among countries. The figures are not concentrated on one side and vary widely throughout all generations.

raw_comment:
1. What do you notice?

It seems that generally young people are more optimistic than the elderly. It could be because of the rapid development that happened these years. For the age between 15 to 24 years old, they were both born in the year around 2000 and experience an extreme pace of development. Could you imagine that 15 years ago most of us are using little smarts and only have the function of communication and now we are using the latest version of the iPhone, Huawei, Samsung with a lot of different functions?  I don't need to explain the convenience that smartphones bring to people's lives, do I? Everyone can see it. With the ability to connect all over the world and share their lives, play games, watch videos......at any time and anywhere, which young people can imagine what life was like before the popularity of the Internet and smartphones decades ago?
This may be a big reason for this phenomenon.
2. What do you wonder?

A 21,000-people survey is relatively small. People aged 25-40 who are neglected should also have the right to express their thoughts. 

3. How does this relate to you and your community? 
Obviously, people in our country did not participate in this survey. Under the great convenience brought to me by the intelligent society, I don't want to give up this change or imagine how inconvenient life seemed 20 or 30 years ago.

4. Make a headline
 Optimistic About the Future?
