Notice:
The points on the left are more than on the right.
There are two groups: ages 15 to 24 years old and adults, ages 40 or older.

Wonder:
People in Brazil and Mali think the world is not getting better with each generation. The percentages of young adults and adults in Brazil are possibly 31% and 12% and in Mali maybe 28% and 24%.
The highest point is in Indonesia, which means people (two age groups) in Indonesia all think the world is getting better.
Overall, young adults are more optimistic than adults.

Relating to the community:
Adults may have more experiences, they saw the questions more clearly and may think the world is not getting better.

What’s going on in this graph? Create a catchy headline that captures the graph’s main idea:
Does each generation have a good or bad view of the world?

What I notice:
- Indonesia is very optimistic for both adults and young adults
- More young adults are optimistic than adults in the US
- Mali is not very optimistic in either categories with around 30% of young adults that are optimistic and 25% of adults that are optimistic.

What I wonder:
* What about the people that are 25-39 year olds? Why isn’t their range + opinions included?
* Why is Indonesia so optimistic? Is it because of their belief, or their schooling, or something else?
* How did they decide which countries to interview? Why wasn’t some countries on the graph, like Thailand or China?
* How come the young adults tend to be more optimistic than adults? Is it because the adults have been there to witness and contribute to the damage of the Earth? Or do the young adults have a more open mind set? Or something else?

I notice that the young adults of first world countries like the US, Japan, and Germany believe that the world is becoming a better place than 40+ year olds in that country. Countries like Indonesia, India, and Nigeria which are not as developed as the US, Japan, or Germany have an equal and high on percentage mix of each generation believing that the world is becoming a better place. 

I wonder why Indonesia is such an optimistic country with the population saying the world is getting better with each generation. I also wonder why the older generations of first world countries don’t believe that the world is becoming a better place than the adults in less developed countries. 

This relates to me because I’m a part of the 15-24 year old generation. I believe that the world is getting better with each generation as many people are learning new ideas and are being exposed to more concepts throughout the world through social media and etc.

raw_comment: [4/5] I wonder if the poll is deemed adequately convincing since the 1,000 respondents on average from each country represent only a tiny portion of the countries’ total population. One thousand people out of the U.K.’s 67 million people is a mere 0.0015%, which cannot precisely tell what the people of the U.K. generally think about this question. In addition, these interviews are not done in person or through video chats, but through telephone calls, which means that respondents don't have to take it seriously. This further takes away the credibility of the polls. While we could argue that covering 1,000 respondents from every country is still a pretty large sample size since it’s not possible to cover everybody or interview everybody in person, we should still leave our doubts on the credibility of the polls. In addition, the poll was asking respondents to address what they think regarding the entire world, but most people might only think about their own country, because that’s what matters most to them and that’s what they care about, but what they think about their own countries do not necessarily have a relationship with whether or not the world is getting better. This potential loophole could also adulterate the authenticity and relevance of the survey results regarding the question. But despite all these accounts, this was still an interesting survey that is worthy of noting and further researching.
ace_sentences: I wonder if the poll is deemed adequately convincing.
The poll has 1,000 respondents on average from each country.
The 1,000 respondents represent only a tiny portion of the countries’ total population.
One thousand people out of the U.K.’s 67 million people is a mere 0.0015%.
This percentage cannot precisely tell what the people of the U.K. generally think about this question.
These interviews are not done in person.
These interviews are not done through video chats.
These interviews are done through telephone calls.
Respondents do not have to take it seriously.
This takes away the credibility of the polls.

It is not possible to cover everybody.
It is not possible to interview everybody in person.
We should still leave our doubts on the credibility of the polls.
The poll was asking respondents to address what they think regarding the entire world.
Most people might only think about their own country.
That is what matters most to them.

This potential loophole could adulterate the relevance of the survey results regarding the question.

This survey is worthy of noting.
This survey is worthy of further researching.

image_description:
### 1) Chart type
- **Scatter plot** (country-labeled points) with a **diagonal reference line** (y = x).

### 2) Layout techniques
- **Single panel** (no faceting/small multiples).
- No stacking; points are positioned in 2D space with **direct text labels**.
- Includes a **reference line** to compare whether values are above/below equality between the two axes.

### 3) Data variables

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| Country | Categorical (nominal) | Each point represents a country; **text label** next to point |
| Percentage of 40+ year-olds who say the world is getting better | Quantitative | **Position on x-axis** |
| Percentage of 15- to 24-year-olds who say the world is getting better | Quantitative | **Position on y-axis** |
| Equality reference (y = x) | Quantitative (derived/reference) | **Diagonal line** across plot indicating equal x and y values |
raw_comment: [2/5] Overall, the number of global and regional crises did not fall but rather got bigger after the Cold War ended in 1991 when a new world order originally endowed with hope was established. Adults who are aged more than 40 have had enough of these havocs, and they have been hoping that things will gradually settle down when the U.S.S.R. collapsed. However, the exact opposite has happened. The disappointment is huge, and so did the hope of having a nonviolent world drop. This should happen to be the answer to why adults aged more than 40 are generally more pessimistic about the current world than young adults aged between 15 to 24 in the poll. These young adults grew up in the best of times with pervasive high-end technologies and democracies, so they have no idea what the quadragenerians have lived through in their childhood and adolescence, which was an era dominated by political animosity between Western democracies and Eastern communist dictatorships, giving rise to a myriad of global conflicts. That explains why younger adults have generally more positive thoughts on the world’s status quo.

raw_comment: [3/5] The second thing I noticed is that there isn’t a clear cause-and-effect relationship between a country’s status quo and the percentage of people in that country who think the world is getting better. Let’s take Brazil and India, who have both been involved in unpleasant domestic situations for the past decade, where Brazil has been struggling to keep the Amazon Rainforest sustainable since the wildfire disaster occurred in 2019, and India has one of the worst covid-19 pandemic situations in the world as of 2021. While both Brazil and India seem to be going through a very rough time, there’s a surprisingly huge gap between the number of Brazilians in the poll who deems that the world has gone better and the number of Indians who deems that the world has gone better. For Brazil, less than 30% of young adults and less than 25% of adults expressed optimism about the world’s status quo, compared to poll takers from India, where more than 60% of young adults and adults expressed optimism about the world’s status quo. Not only India but also countries such as the U.S., Ukraine, and Lebanon have also undergone rough times in recent years, but have more than half of its respondents expressed positive views regarding the poll question. This is an interesting phenomenon that is worthy of further investigation.

raw_comment: [1/5] The first thing I noticed is the sharp variance between the percentage of adults and young adults who think the world is becoming better where the former age group has a more negative view regarding the issue than the latter age group for 18 of the 21 countries selected for the poll. In my opinion, this discrepancy happens because adults who are older than 40-year-old have more knowledge and experience about how the world has changed over the past half-century, as to young adults who have only witnessed the change over the past one or two decades, so older adults usually possess more visual and tangible opinions to whether or not the world is getting better with each generation. In the contemporary world, we are facing many crises ranging from the most urgent public health concerns such as the Ebola Outbreak, the exacerbating HIV/AIDS epidemics, and the worldwide covid-19 pandemic, to wars and unrests such as fighting and skirmishes in Afghanistan, Iraq, and Ukraine, political mayhems embroiling over Myanmar, Egypt, Hong Kong, Algeria, Pakistan, Afghanistan, Iran, Iraq, the United States, the United Kingdom, as well as the surging cases of terrorist attacks and hate crimes all over the world both in developing countries and developed countries, and finally to an increased number of natural disasters caused by global warming and the cumulative over-exploitation of the environment, such as forest fires in the Amazon rainforest, Australian desert, and Taiwan.

- Also, I notice that some big country such as India and US, and Brazil doesn't have a high percentage of thinking that the world is getting better with each generation.

image_description:
### 1) Chart type
- **Scatter plot** (country-labeled points) with a **diagonal reference line** (y = x).

### 2) Layout techniques
- **Single panel** (no faceting/small multiples).
- No stacking; points are positioned in 2D space with **direct text labels**.
- Includes a **reference line** to compare whether values are above/below equality between the two axes.

### 3) Data variables

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| Country | Categorical (nominal) | Each point represents a country; **text label** next to point |
| Percentage of 40+ year-olds who say the world is getting better | Quantitative | **Position on x-axis** |
| Percentage of 15- to 24-year-olds who say the world is getting better | Quantitative | **Position on y-axis** |
| Equality reference (y = x) | Quantitative (derived/reference) | **Diagonal line** across plot indicating equal x and y values |
raw_comment:
What I notice:
It is apparent that in most of the countries, 15-to 24-year-olds people are more likely to satisfy with the progress of improving the world than 40+year-olds people. The countries located close to the black line like Indonesia, India, Nigeria, Bangladesh, Morocco, and Mali have relatively less difference in thoughts between generations. Overall, relatively fewer people in Brazil, and Mali are satisfied with the world, and have a hopeful view of the future, whereas people in Indonesia are more hopeful about the progress for the past few years no matter which generation they’re in. Also, the countries possessing a higher percentage of satisfaction regardless of generation are not fully developed countries. The developed countries that lead the world’s economy are mostly located in the middle part like the US, Germany, the UK.
 
What I wonder: 
I wonder about the specific structure or environment of a country that impacts people to build the view of the society. For example, I wonder how the society in the United States influences the younger generation to get a positive view of the world, whereas the older generation tends to view the future negatively though they’re living at the same time.

Relation to my community:
As the graph shows, the society that I spent most of my lives in had a more positive view of the world among the younger generation, rather than 40+.

 A catchy headline that captures the graph’s main idea:
Is the world indeed getting better?

"The Generation of 2021 COVID TIMES!"

First I think that the world is not getting better with each generation I believe because all of social media is just changing who people are in good and bad ways but I personally tend to see only the bad ways. For example people who are young tend to wear clothing that shows a lot of skin thinking that they are an adult and feeling mature. From my eyes it looks like they are trying to impress others with their riches and fame. My mom told me that when you see someone dance you can tell if they are dancing cause they love to or just dancing to impress others. When they are trying to impress others it feels desperate. Another example is body image, I feel like what you see online will make you try it, which can really hurt you physically and mentally.

4. What’s going on in this graph? 
- I feel like the graph is showing different opinions that people in different countries have about life and also similar opinions that people have with each other. I feel like that this can really affect people and their life.

raw_comment:
"The Opinions of Generations!"

First of all the most noticeable thing on the graph is that more 15-24-year-olds voted than 40+ year-olds. Another point to be made is within the 15-24-year-olds category there are many more countries and many of them are more developed compared to the countries in the 40+ year-olds category. This leaves me wondering why these countries have the response they do and why these questions were targeted specifically towards these generations. As a member of the 15-24-year-old category, I am exposed to many online sources for me to access information on the latest political and social news happening in all parts of the world. I feel as if this access to daily events gives me an advantage and a more open-minded opinion on this matter. 40+ year-olds come from a very different generation and have been raised differently and would most likely not have the knowledge or access to the internet as other generations who use it in their daily lives. Many 40+ year-olds do have the knowledge and are also exposed to the media but in a different way to how we use it. As to what is happening in the graph I believe a majority of 15-24-year-olds said that the world is getting better with each generation because we are the ones who will be living in it for a longer time and molding it into our own world. I trust the results of this graph even though I may disagree with them however, I support UNICEF so I am interested in discovering the reason behind these results.

I wonder why Indonesia’s results are so high and why Brazil and Mali's results are so low. I wonder if their location impacted their results. I also wonder if these results will change in the future or how these results would have compared to the past.

raw_comment:
Title: Boomers are a Downer!

This graph shows that, at a global level, young people are more optimistic about the world than older people.  There are a few places where - by a narrow margin - older people are more positive, but it is more common for young people to be substantially more optimistic.  Japan and the US stand out as having round about a 40% gap between proportions of young and old saying the world is getting better.  This is an interesting counterpoint to many discourses around generational attitudes: especially the notion that GenZ is somehow more 'cynical', and also the evidence that the younger generation has a much worse time than the baby boomers when it comes to job security and home ownership prospects.
